title: Phase N.1 Unified Critical Thinking Bridge and Toulmin Studio Routes
description: OS1 to OS2 translation routes for public EECSL cases.
promptGovernance: The prompts and questions on this page are examples, suggestions, opportunities, or explorations. They are not automatic graded assignments. Instructors decide which prompts, if any, students should use, adapt, submit, discuss, or ignore.
studentPrivacy: Do not enter private personal, family, medical, legal, employment, or financial information into public tools. Public tools are for shared case analysis. Personal application belongs only in private course spaces when your instructor directs it.

routes.os1: This institution seems fair or unfair; something about opportunity, debt, support, or extraction feels morally important.
routes.claim: A public-facing institution can satisfy formal access requirements while still failing to provide agency-supporting capabilities.
grounds: IPS categories compare extraction pressure, entropy/burden, regulatory routing, residue, and misalignment.
The case lets students analyze public institutional patterns without private disclosure.
routes.warrant: Formal opportunity does not equal real capability when institutional structure shifts burdens onto vulnerable students.
routes.qualifier: This is a teaching proxy and requires careful source verification before becoming a reviewer-facing empirical claim.
routes.rebuttal: Some observed outcomes may reflect background inequality, student choice, or external labor-market conditions rather than institutional design alone.
routes.repair: Move from blame-only language to T1/T2 repair: structural transparency, support capacity, debt burden reduction, and honest mission alignment.

routes.os1: The crisis feels like greed, betrayal, deception, or irresponsibility.
routes.claim: The crisis involved institutionalized risk transfer and evidence-policy failure, not merely individual moral weakness.
grounds: Risk was packaged, rated, sold, and externalized through complex financial instruments.
Warnings and uncertainty were normalized or overridden by incentives and market confidence.
routes.warrant: Institutions that privatize gains while socializing risks violate commons-governance responsibilities.
routes.qualifier: Not every actor had equal knowledge, power, intention, or causal responsibility.
routes.rebuttal: Some losses may have resulted from uncertainty or distributed system dynamics rather than deliberate deception by every participant.
routes.repair: Strengthen evidence-channel integrity, accountability for risk externalization, and governance of shared-risk systems.

routes.os1: The case may feel wrong because confinement, profit, vulnerability, and public responsibility are entangled.
routes.claim: A prison-adjacent public-private institution must be evaluated by how it handles captive vulnerability, not only by contract performance.
grounds: Exit options are constrained or absent.
Costs, burdens, accountability, and repair may be distributed across public and private systems.
routes.warrant: Agency is ethically fragile when persons affected by a system cannot meaningfully exit, contest, or repair the conditions shaping them.
routes.qualifier: The argument must distinguish verified evidence from teaching-proxy simulation output.
routes.rebuttal: Some public-private arrangements may claim efficiency or capacity benefits that must be evaluated rather than dismissed in advance.
routes.repair: Ask what transparency, oversight, burden tracking, voice protection, and non-extractive incentives would be required.

routes.case: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite
routes.os1: The case may feel disturbing because care, vulnerability, ownership opacity, and profit are combined.
routes.claim: Care institutions can become ethically parasitic when extraction is hidden behind formal service delivery and vulnerable persons cannot see or contest the ledger.
grounds: Ownership and cost structures can hide where value flows.
Residents, families, and frontline workers may bear risks they cannot easily measure or escape.
routes.warrant: Care systems have heightened duties because dependency and vulnerability reduce ordinary exit and bargaining power.
routes.qualifier: Not every ownership or management structure produces the same outcomes; careful evidence comparison is required.
routes.rebuttal: Some financial or management arrangements may improve capacity if transparency, quality, and accountability are strong.
routes.repair: Make hidden burdens visible, protect voice, track care-quality consequences, and shift from appearance of compliance to agency-supporting repair.

lenses.name: Socrates
lenses.notices: Confusion, unexamined claims, overconfidence, and the gap between appearing wise and being wise.
lenses.claimFrame: A person or institution should not act as if a moral issue is settled until its assumptions have been examined.
lenses.warrant: Ethical agency requires examined belief because unexamined certainty can become irresponsible action.
lenses.worksWhen: Students need to slow down, define terms, expose hidden assumptions, or move from slogans to inquiry.
lenses.failsWhen: Urgent protective action is required and endless questioning becomes avoidance.

lenses.name: Aristotle
lenses.notices: Character formation, habits, flourishing, institutions that cultivate or deform practical judgment.
lenses.claimFrame: The case should be judged partly by the kind of agents and habits the system forms over time.
lenses.warrant: Ethical systems matter because repeated practices shape character, perception, and practical wisdom.
lenses.worksWhen: The case concerns professional formation, institutional culture, role habits, courage, or judgment under pressure.
lenses.failsWhen: Structural coercion is so strong that individual virtue language hides institutional responsibility.

lenses.name: Kant
lenses.notices: Dignity, consent, deception, universalizable rules, and treating persons as ends rather than mere means.
lenses.claimFrame: The system is ethically defective when it uses persons as instruments for institutional gain without respecting their agency.
lenses.warrant: Persons have dignity and should not be reduced to tools for profit, convenience, or risk transfer.
lenses.worksWhen: The case involves deception, coercion, consent failure, or exploitation of vulnerable persons.
lenses.failsWhen: The case requires fine-grained outcome comparison or tradeoff analysis beyond rule-focused judgment.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite

lenses.name: Bentham / Mill
lenses.notices: Aggregate harm, benefit, risk distribution, preventable suffering, and long-term consequences.
lenses.claimFrame: The case should be evaluated by the balance and distribution of harms and benefits it produces.
lenses.warrant: Ethical evaluation must account for real consequences, especially preventable suffering and systemic risk.
lenses.worksWhen: Students need to compare harms, benefits, risk transfer, and policy outcomes.
lenses.failsWhen: Aggregate benefit language hides rights violations or sacrifices vulnerable minorities.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite

lenses.name: Martha Nussbaum
lenses.notices: The gap between formal rights and real agency-supporting conditions.
lenses.claimFrame: Formal access is insufficient if people lack the actual capabilities needed to use that access meaningfully.
lenses.warrant: Justice requires real opportunities for functioning, not merely formal permission or abstract rights.
lenses.worksWhen: The case concerns education, health, care, disability, poverty, vulnerability, or institutional exclusion.
lenses.failsWhen: Capabilities are listed abstractly without attention to local agency, culture, and implementation.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite

lenses.name: John Rawls
lenses.notices: Basic fairness, institutional design, arbitrary advantage, and whether rules could be accepted from behind a veil of ignorance.
lenses.claimFrame: The institutional arrangement is unjust if rational persons would reject it without knowing their place within it.
lenses.warrant: Just institutions should be defensible to persons who do not know whether they will be advantaged or vulnerable within the system.
lenses.worksWhen: The case requires evaluating institutional rules, burdens, access, fairness, and public justification.
lenses.failsWhen: Formal fairness language overlooks relational care, historical exclusion, or lived trauma.

lenses.name: Care Ethics
lenses.notices: Dependency, unequal burden, voice, care labor, abandonment, and relational repair.
lenses.claimFrame: The case is ethically defective when it hides or exploits dependency while failing to sustain responsible care relationships.
lenses.warrant: Moral life depends on networks of care, vulnerability, attention, and responsiveness.
lenses.worksWhen: The case centers care, aging, illness, education, families, professional dependency, or frontline labor.
lenses.failsWhen: Care language becomes sentimental and does not address power, law, money, or institutional design.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite

lenses.name: Wangari Maathai
lenses.tradition: Functional Equivalence / Environmental and Civic Repair
lenses.notices: Shared practical tasks that different communities can endorse for different authentic reasons.
lenses.claimFrame: Coordination can be ethically powerful when diverse agents support the same repair task without requiring worldview agreement.
lenses.warrant: Functional equivalence allows OS2 cooperation across PRF differences when a shared task protects agency-supporting conditions.
lenses.worksWhen: The case needs civic coordination, repair, ecological thinking, or cross-community cooperation.
lenses.failsWhen: Shared-task language hides coercion, unequal burden, or suppressed dissent.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite

lenses.name: Miranda Fricker
lenses.notices: Whose knowledge is heard, dismissed, mistrusted, or made unintelligible by institutional patterns.
lenses.claimFrame: The system is ethically defective when relevant testimony or experience is discounted because of identity, status, role, or institutional convenience.
lenses.warrant: Justice requires fair participation in knowledge practices, not merely fair distribution of material goods.
lenses.worksWhen: The case concerns testimony, warning, professional voice, marginalized knowledge, or credibility gaps.
lenses.failsWhen: Epistemic injustice is treated only as interpersonal disrespect rather than as institutional structure.
caseUse: Florida Nursing Homes and the Visible Parasite
